title: Terrible monetization
text:
This app is nearly unusable after the last few updates. It used to be ad supported, but now the ad player is buggy, annoying, and frequently crashes, all to push people to subscribe to Super Duolingo. It’s unfortunate that such a great app has been ruined by such a greedy monetization scheme, but at this point it isn’t even worth using anymore.

Avoid. There are better options out there.

title: Changed for the worse
text: This app has made several updates and changes while I have had it. Initially it used to take lessons slow and methodical, it included small introductions on the why’s and how’s for language patterns, every unit had an alphabet lesson to learn that along with the language, and it gave enough gems to perform legendary levels if you used the app consistently.  Now they have taken away the introductions, taken away the mandatory alphabet lessons, changed out the hearts for energy where you can’t even finish a legendary while getting every answer right forcing you to give up or spend money on more energy. It turned from a good language learning app to a mediocre cash grab. And now, it won’t even give me all of the XP I earn from completing lessons. I just finished a lesson with double XP putting me at 118 points, and then when I checked my standing directly after I was at 86 points right back into the demotion zone. This is making the app less and less worth using, especially with all of the existing alternatives out there these days. Please fix the app, stop taking our points, stop taking away useful features, and stop making the app less functional just to squeeze money out of us.

title: Mixed feelings about this app
text: English Duolingo has some real issues. English is my first language, and I received advanced placement in English before going to college, so I believe I speak English properly with regard to grammar and so forth. I have at times used the English module to gain points rapidly so that I don’t fall into the “demotion zone.” When asked to repeat a sentence, Duolingo often acts as though you are not pronouncing contractions. Sometimes the app will hesitate for 20 or 30 seconds before accepting your response. Sometimes you are asked to fill in a blank where the first two letters are provided and on a number of occasions, I have filled in a correct answer, but it wasn’t the exact answer according to Duolingo and I was marked wrong. There are some other mistakes in the app as well. These detract from my enjoyment, and I think might sometimes interfere with a foreign language-speaking person’s ability to learn English using this app.

title: Going downhill a bit..
text:
I’ve learned a bit from Duolingo, it’s always been accessible to me, even without any membership, different to so many language learning apps.
I logged on for the first time in a while, and saw that instead of hearts, there’s energy. Before, you could complete as many lessons as you wanted, so long as you didn’t run out of hearts. Now, you’re limited. 
I got unlimited energy for 5 minutes, but then I completed a lesson, and it took 2 energies away, and takes 2 hours to refill. 
And smart marketing strategy with making the video call option as part of the lesson. By habit of using the app, people are more likely to tap the top button to complete a typical lesson. On the video call tiles, the option to buy unlimited is at the top, therefore increasing the chance that someone will press on it accidentally.
Definitely annoying. 
Is your huge meme base not enough? The downloads? The ads? I understand revenue, but this is starting to be annoying, and is heading in the direction of every other language learning app on the market. Not real innovative, Duo.

title: Mixed feelings about this app
text: English Duolingo has some real issues. English is my first language, and I received advanced placement in English before going to college, so I believe I speak English properly with regard to grammar and so forth. I have at times used the English module to gain points rapidly so that I don’t fall into the “demotion zone.” When asked to repeat a sentence, Duolingo often acts as though you are not pronouncing contractions. Sometimes the app will hesitate for 20 or 30 seconds before accepting your response. Sometimes you are asked to fill in a blank where the first two letters are provided and on a number of occasions, I have filled in a correct answer, but it wasn’t the exact answer according to Duolingo and I was marked wrong. There are some other mistakes in the app as well. These detract from my enjoyment, and I think might sometimes interfere with a foreign language-speaking person’s ability to learn English using this app.

title: Going downhill a bit..
text:
I’ve learned a bit from Duolingo, it’s always been accessible to me, even without any membership, different to so many language learning apps.
I logged on for the first time in a while, and saw that instead of hearts, there’s energy. Before, you could complete as many lessons as you wanted, so long as you didn’t run out of hearts. Now, you’re limited. 
I got unlimited energy for 5 minutes, but then I completed a lesson, and it took 2 energies away, and takes 2 hours to refill. 
And smart marketing strategy with making the video call option as part of the lesson. By habit of using the app, people are more likely to tap the top button to complete a typical lesson. On the video call tiles, the option to buy unlimited is at the top, therefore increasing the chance that someone will press on it accidentally.
Definitely annoying. 
Is your huge meme base not enough? The downloads? The ads? I understand revenue, but this is starting to be annoying, and is heading in the direction of every other language learning app on the market. Not real innovative, Duo.

title: Content going downhill
text:
2025

The content is getting worse.   From what I can tell, they have made great effort in integrating AI, and made no effort in correcting or checking the mistakes AI made.  Due to the unmonitored content, I have unsubscribed from their paid plans. 

1. The forum/comments feature has been removed and thus there is no interaction with other students. Duolingo, of all your talk about pedagogy, peer learning helps with explanation and correction.  The AI explanation is narrowly limited to the grammar of the sentence and does NOT help explain why phrases are used in other circumstances, which the forum did. 
2. There is been no correction of the errors I have reported, errors which I had confirmed with a native speaker. 
3. Many spoken words had their final syllable clipped off.  It was obviously that the automated sound editing decided that the syllable was unimportant.  
4. AI Lily doesn't wait for me to think, and neither does it understand my stuttering sentences.  Even Siri understands my sentences.  AI Lily also speaks in code, "asterisk asterisk word asterisk asterisk", obviously a **word** which Duolingo has programmed for the AI to use. 
5. The listening comprehension 'radio' exercises has a good format, but I am again let down by the errors in the content.  These were errors which cannot be reported (and thus not corrected). Errors such as defaulting to the wrong gender, or asking a question on something not mentioned in the conversation.  I suspect that these were generated by AI without a human gatekeeper. 

2022

Duolingo has effectively drilled me for a year, and my friend said that I can get by in their country. The tips for each unit are practical, but for those learning a language formally, these tips do not address grammar rules. Oftentimes, the forum conversation are more helpful in their explanation. 

 Things I would like improvement on: 

1. Have a way for mobile users to participate in the forums conversations. 

2. The new path interface (v2022-11) is a let down usability-wise. Granted that the learning was streamlined, but one now has no quick visual indication of what each unit covers, and has to randomly tap on each step to reveal the unit name.  This new UI borrowed too much from single-goal games, and lost an essential function of learning, which is to give the learners autonomy over what they want to learn.  The new UI also wastes a lot screen real estate, particularly on mobile devices. A user has to scroll through screens and screens to get to what they (then have to guess is the topic they) want.

title: misleading pet info in readings
text: I was reading the French story about Bea recommending Eddy to buy hamsters for Junior. It mentioned "with two hamsters, they are less lonely" which is very misleading and harmful. Hamsters are solitary animals. They will be stressed out when there are multiple in a household. If two were in cage, even a male and a female, it is likely this would end in one dead+half eaten and one badly wounded. Please change your story content.

title: Content going downhill
text:
2025

The content is getting worse.   From what I can tell, they have made great effort in integrating AI, and made no effort in correcting or checking the mistakes AI made.  Due to the unmonitored content, I have unsubscribed from their paid plans. 

1. The forum/comments feature has been removed and thus there is no interaction with other students. Duolingo, of all your talk about pedagogy, peer learning helps with explanation and correction.  The AI explanation is narrowly limited to the grammar of the sentence and does NOT help explain why phrases are used in other circumstances, which the forum did. 
2. There is been no correction of the errors I have reported, errors which I had confirmed with a native speaker. 
3. Many spoken words had their final syllable clipped off.  It was obviously that the automated sound editing decided that the syllable was unimportant.  
4. AI Lily doesn't wait for me to think, and neither does it understand my stuttering sentences.  Even Siri understands my sentences.  AI Lily also speaks in code, "asterisk asterisk word asterisk asterisk", obviously a **word** which Duolingo has programmed for the AI to use. 
5. The listening comprehension 'radio' exercises has a good format, but I am again let down by the errors in the content.  These were errors which cannot be reported (and thus not corrected). Errors such as defaulting to the wrong gender, or asking a question on something not mentioned in the conversation.  I suspect that these were generated by AI without a human gatekeeper. 

2022

Duolingo has effectively drilled me for a year, and my friend said that I can get by in their country. The tips for each unit are practical, but for those learning a language formally, these tips do not address grammar rules. Oftentimes, the forum conversation are more helpful in their explanation. 

 Things I would like improvement on: 

1. Have a way for mobile users to participate in the forums conversations. 

2. The new path interface (v2022-11) is a let down usability-wise. Granted that the learning was streamlined, but one now has no quick visual indication of what each unit covers, and has to randomly tap on each step to reveal the unit name.  This new UI borrowed too much from single-goal games, and lost an essential function of learning, which is to give the learners autonomy over what they want to learn.  The new UI also wastes a lot screen real estate, particularly on mobile devices. A user has to scroll through screens and screens to get to what they (then have to guess is the topic they) want.

First, I liked the heart system, but I’ve gotten used to the energy one.

Second, and my main thing. I can’t scroll down during lessons. I’m using my phone, and one of those problems comes up where it’s a long dialogue and there are three options. And I can ONLY SEE TWO. This could just be a phone thing but it’s most likely a Duo bug. So if you could fix it, that would be great

title: Downfall
text: I hate what Duolingo has become. I used to use the app religiously, it was an integral part of my daily routine. In the last few months it’s become nothing but over the top haptics and unnecessary visuals every other question. Sure, you can turn the haptics off but that doesn’t excuse the over-saturation of animations and visuals and they just keep adding more. It’s as though they’re taking a page from slot-machines in Vegas and making sure something changes visually at least every 2-3 seconds to hold your attention because they know otherwise their app is falling flat and they’re desperate to keep users engaged any way they can. It’s gotten so bad I’ve lost all interest in keeping the 1300+ day streak I worked so hard for at one point. And don’t even get me started on their recent “AI first” approach.

title: Going downhill
text: I have loved using Duolingo to learn Spanish for many years and I currently pay for a family account for my parents and sister. However, I have seen a significant decrease in the quality of the content over the past year. The units are so short now you barely learn anything about each topic. The voices of the characters have changed and have less intonation, making the listening practice less useful. The translations and corrections often have mistakes, which I had never seen happen before. It seems like the whole app is being run by AI, and it’s hard to really learn a language from a computer. After the years I’ve spend on Duolingo I would hate to give up on the app, but if I can find a language program that hasn’t handed all of the lesson creation over to AI I will cancel my Duolingo subscription.

title: Alright for brushing up on Romance languages but that’s about it
text: This app is alright if, say, you studied French in school and want to brush up before a trip to Paris. But if you are trying to actually learn a new language, or are interested in a language that is derived from Latin, look elsewhere. The format of the lessons is not conducive to learning a language from scratch, as there is no explanation of the grammar or translation of new vocabulary. You’re basically just guessing as you go along. Also, for languages like Hebrew, with a grammatical structure that is very different from English or Romance languages, I often answer a question correctly, but because of the linguistic nuance the answer is considered incorrect. This is especially true for expressed rather than inflected languages where the word placement is less rigid (ex: I translate a sentence “this boy is short” when the app wanted “this is a short boy” and I am penalized even though both translations are correct). Incredibly frustrating.

title: Terrible app
text: I have to keep deducting stars from my review, because this app is declining in quality to the point where it’s unusable. After numerous unfair things this app has done, I would give it -100,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 stars if I could, and I will continue to update my negative review when this app does something that is antithetical to learning and an attempt to steal money and gems. Sometimes the audio is wrong and skips words, so when you don’t type words that aren’t actually said, you’re marked wrong, even though the exercise tells you to type what you hear. If the audio skips words I won’t type them. Considering you are asked to type, say, and translate sentences that make no sense and would never say, it is understandable that Duolingo would completely skip words. It still marks you incorrect for correct translations, or it marks you wrong and then when you make the correction later, it tells you you’re wrong again and that the correct answer is what you put last time. This has happened numerous times in the French and Spanish lessons, even when you follow the example and lesson from the tips they make you look at before proceeding. It tests you on things that it never taught, and the hints are either useless or wrong. For example, one sentence required two “las,” but the hint only included one, so it marked me wrong. I relied on hints instead of my gut for three questions in a French lesson and was marked wrong. It also wants you to answer in a way that is not what the lesson is about. For example, in a Spanish lesson called “using the past progressive tense,” I used the past progressive tense correctly. However, it marked me wrong and used the imperfect indicative tense in its “correct” answer. Additionally, there are different ways to use object pronouns in Spanish. You can say “te lo quiero mandar” or “quiero mandártelo,” the latter being more efficient. In French, instead of “Tu fais du thé?” for “Are you making tea?” it wanted me to say “Tu es en train de faire du thé?” which was never taught and inefficient, just so that it could steal 600 gems from me. I answered numerous questions correctly and was marked wrong anyway and had to spend 725 gems to refill my hearts, even though I shouldn’t have lost any hearts at all. It marked me wrong twice for using “dormitorio” instead of “habitación,” even though both words mean “bedroom” in Spanish and both words were included in the hints. And guess which word was used multiple times in the next lesson? You guessed it: dormitorio! It just didn’t want me to get 90% or above correct so that I could earn gems. The same happened when I answered everything correctly, but I was marked wrong on 3 exercises so that I could get a 79% instead of an 80% and not earn gems. It marks correct answers incorrect because it wants you to pay for unlimited hearts, not learn and use the language correctly. Sometimes the keyboard doesn’t work properly and will put in letters that you didn’t type or won’t let you use accent marks and then mark you wrong for misspelling it. The hints are constantly wrong and useless, because you get marked wrong for using the hints. It’s clear that Duolingo just wants you to lose hearts so that you pay for unlimited hearts (which I will never do, because I refuse to be conned into paying for something, and my payment won’t go toward improving the app) or miss out on earning gems for perfect lessons. It would be nice if they gave you a heart every time they accept a suggestion you make. I click on “my answer should have been accepted” and get emails saying that my suggestion is now an acceptable answer (although it’s been awhile), but I don’t get any type of reward for doing their job for them and knowing something that they don’t. Or they should let you keep a heart if you report the question for something going wrong, like their hints being incorrect. Otherwise, they will just make you lose hearts for no good reason so that you lose streaks, miss out on rewards, or pay unnecessarily. I have to use Google translate or take screenshots of my Duolingo screen in order to keep hearts, even though I’m not actually getting anything wrong, so that I put what Duolingo wants me to put and not an equally correct answer. On two occasions I had to redo an entire lesson that I completed perfectly for no reason. I saw the progress bar go up and then reset. Duolingo should be happy that people are learning and answering questions correctly. Instead, they pull some nonsense so that they can get money. Now the stories and listening tasks make you answer questions that have nothing to do with the story. None of the answer choices are correct, so it’s just a way for them to take hearts away, because I have to click answers at random. I also never get the chests I win in order to boost XP. I used to get promoted every week until I reached the top, and now I’m always in danger of getting demoted, even though I do as many lessons as before, sometimes more. I have to say things correctly numerous times for me to move on in the lesson because the voice recognition is terrible. I’ve updated my review numerous times with new complaints about this app, as well as sent emails, and I haven’t received a single developer response. I also get no response to my emails, just an automated acknowledgment that they received it. I can’t continue my lesson because I get an error message. It prompts me to contact app support, but the site just says “loading.” This is just more proof that Duolingo is run by greedy cheaters who hate when their users are intelligent and learning things.

title: Ads are brutal
text: Coming back to the free version of the app after a year is like turning the difficulty up to hard mode. When I used the free version a year ago, they had a heart system where you could make the mistakes before you failed a lesson. It was simple because you would know how many mistakes you could make ahead of time and you could watch one ad to regain one heart. Now, they have the less predictable (and punishing) energy system. In this system, you can have up to 25 energy stored. Every answer in a lesson, regardless of right or wrong, consumes energy. If you run out of energy during a lesson you have to quit or spend gems. Sometimes you’ll be awarded extra energy in a lesson, but not always. Watching an ad between lessons restores 5 energy (you have to watch five ads to refill your energy from zero). I hate this system because often times, making a Single mistake, in a lesson I started with full energy, means I will run out of energy before the end, and have to watch FIVE advertisements to refill my energy before the next attempt. I hate how aggressive the advertising has become so much that most days I’ll just do one lesson to continue my streak and continue learning in another app. If you don’t plan on paying for premium, save yourself a lot of time, and ad watching, and either go find a better app or take a class at a local community college instead.

You need people (especially native speakers) to be able to learn a language in full. title: Alright for brushing up on Romance languages but that’s about it
text: This app is alright if, say, you studied French in school and want to brush up before a trip to Paris. But if you are trying to actually learn a new language, or are interested in a language that is derived from Latin, look elsewhere. The format of the lessons is not conducive to learning a language from scratch, as there is no explanation of the grammar or translation of new vocabulary. You’re basically just guessing as you go along. Also, for languages like Hebrew, with a grammatical structure that is very different from English or Romance languages, I often answer a question correctly, but because of the linguistic nuance the answer is considered incorrect. This is especially true for expressed rather than inflected languages where the word placement is less rigid (ex: I translate a sentence “this boy is short” when the app wanted “this is a short boy” and I am penalized even though both translations are correct). Incredibly frustrating.

The changing of the heart system to the stupid energy one, the insane amount of push for free trials, encouragement to nag your friends to keep using it, not to mention the leagues. I swear half the time the leagues don’t even have real people in them just to make it seem like you’re losing and need to play more. 
I used to think a lesson a day would be good but this is ridiculous, I can’t even complete one without some sort of psychological tactic at getting me to give them money. If you can manage to stay free-use good for you, but if you’re gonna spend money on a subscription don’t give it to these greedy owls.

title: Learning Korean
text: This has really helped me learn Korean so I can understand K-pop and when people speak in Korean.

title: Great for intro, surprisingly poor grammatical and structural teaching
text: Very well-developed introductory lessons for learning vocabulary and building comfort speaking. Once irregular verbs, conjugation, and grammatical sentences structure become important for lessons, Duo feels like it throws you into construct creation with trial and error examples without the proper orientation to the underlying rules that govern each response. It has been frustrating to watch each of my children lose interest as they are confronted with repeated failures because they were not provided the scaffolding necessary to understand the constructions, and I long for the tables of conjugation that would get me through this important phase as I was trying to learn with them. Duo, please put the same amount of creativity and explanation into instruction around reflexive verbs, conjugation, and irregular verbs that you do with early content so that we’re not trying to memorize phrases through brute force of multiple failures.

title: Energy is a change for the worst
text: If Apple would allow 0 star ratings this app would be worthy of it. I’ve been using Duolingo for 9 years. There have been a lot of mildly irritating things introduced over the years (hearts, ads, single path lessons, forcing you to follow strangers to meet an unlisted criteria to have friends  quests…) but this energy bar is by far the worst change I’ve seen with the app. Maybe less time should be spent developing blatant money grab attempts and more time should be spent fixing errors with your app (e.g. path progression being frozen by completing certain types of lessons, awful recognition for speaking exercises, mistakes in the courses)

title: Built for Retention, Not Learning
text: Almost to a 800 day streak, and I haven't learned anything. I really liked this app because it's great at keeping you motivated and its competitiveness/streak keeps you wanting to play. But the more I've used this, the more I've seen how the app has changed... and it's not for the better. They're constantly lowering the bar and accepting the bare minimum. The things they teach are enough to finish a lesson but not enough to actually retain the information (and don't even bother trying to learn grammar with this app.) Why don't we have more flash-card type lessons where we learn words? I'm not learning anything with the words spoon-fed to me. And why aren't pronouns being taught with words?! Cards should say "die Katze" not "Katze." (Same with teaching cases) Match Madness is fun and easy, but having a time limit depletes the purpose imo. Instead of giving the answer right in front of you, you should have to type out the word without reference. Or have similar words to pick from instead of making guessing easy. I also wish there was an option for this app to teach you just what you need to travel. I got this years ago to prep for a vacation, and I think I could only ask where the bathroom is after 800 days. Sigh. After I go to Germany this year, I'll probably delete the app and buy something else that actually focuses on learning. (Just can't give up the streak quite yet!)

text:
EDIT: Changed my score to 1 star…I think AI is destroying this app. I just completed Section 3 Unit 20 for the Italian course, “Use the Future of Andare”. Literally only the very last question in the Unit Review exercise used the future tense of Andare, and only one other question used that verb at all, out of 14 or 15 questions. Pathetic.

I’ve been using this app for three years (currently have a 477-day streak), and I really enjoy it and it has helped me learn Italian (in conjunction with living and working in an Italian-speaking area during the summer). However, the recent change from Hearts (deducted when you make a mistake) to Energy (deducted every time you answer a question but randomly replenished by a small amount) is awful. It means I can no longer use it for more than say 10-15 minutes in a day without watching twice as many ads as before. I don’t even know if watching an additional ad would replenish enough energy to compete another exercise, and I don’t plan to find out. Because of this change I will avoid watching ads as much as possible on this app. No more extra gems for me. Awful cash-grab from a developer who was already tainting their quality app with AI-generated content.

title: Pros and cons
text:
This app is pretty good for learning languages.  I’ve noticed a fair number of mispronunciations in Russian, though.  Sometimes it’s just wrong for the word, but other times it actually results in the wrong word(stand vs stay, for instance; exact same spelling, but different pronunciations).

Also, I think the learning stands - or should stand - on its own.  This app had gotten really gamey lately, and it’s rather annoying.   Like the radio show bit, late night with…, etc.  I get that some people may like these things, and maybe they even help people, but can you please make them optional?  While I’m on that subject, can you remove from the progression path any of the things that require a higher paid subscription?  I already pay, but not at the tier to open certain things (that I honestly wouldn’t enjoy, anyway), so those things just clutter up my progression path.

I’ve also noticed that you can only share once to Facebook per day how well you are doing.  “You got 85% in Russian!  Share to Facebook?”  Once you do, it doesn’t allow it for any other language you may take lessons in for the day, when you used to be able to for numerous languages.  Small issue, I suppose, but I wish you would bring it back.  

All these things combined lead me to leave a two-star rating.  I thought about three, but I just can’t do it.  Please consider these issues. 

Signed,

Long-time, perturbed subscriber

title: Pros and cons
text:
This app is pretty good for learning languages.  I’ve noticed a fair number of mispronunciations in Russian, though.  Sometimes it’s just wrong for the word, but other times it actually results in the wrong word(stand vs stay, for instance; exact same spelling, but different pronunciations).

Also, I think the learning stands - or should stand - on its own.  This app had gotten really gamey lately, and it’s rather annoying.   Like the radio show bit, late night with…, etc.  I get that some people may like these things, and maybe they even help people, but can you please make them optional?  While I’m on that subject, can you remove from the progression path any of the things that require a higher paid subscription?  I already pay, but not at the tier to open certain things (that I honestly wouldn’t enjoy, anyway), so those things just clutter up my progression path.

I’ve also noticed that you can only share once to Facebook per day how well you are doing.  “You got 85% in Russian!  Share to Facebook?”  Once you do, it doesn’t allow it for any other language you may take lessons in for the day, when you used to be able to for numerous languages.  Small issue, I suppose, but I wish you would bring it back.  

All these things combined lead me to leave a two-star rating.  I thought about three, but I just can’t do it.  Please consider these issues. 

Signed,

Long-time, perturbed subscriber

I’m not being paid for this

 Chess, is another story. Yes ive learned a lot in the past 3 weeks but ELO barely goes up if you win against Oscar, but if “Chess Daddy” (a recent phrase theyve added) beats YOU, the ELO goes down IMMENSELY. 

If you want to learn chess, you need to have patience. 

Super Duolingo/ Duolingo Max IS EXPENSIVE; but also worth it as you can do really anything with it. 

THANK YOU! ☘️

title: Do not waste your time!
text:
I have done Duolingo for 2 years,  I’ve used it 5-15 minutes’s a day, I can understand some words & can order food in a restaurant, but my friend used a different app for 6 months & could communicate WAY BETTER THAN ME. 
I came home & switched & now I can not only understand the lyrics of Mexican music after 6 months, I can actually have conversations, I could have never had with Duolingo. 
I feel like I wasted 2 years of my life for nothing. Trust me, use Jumpspeak instead.

title: new update is annoying, also some confusing lessons
text:
So I have a 500+ day streak, I’ve been on Duolingo for years but haven’t felt the need to really complain until now. I don’t know what the deal is with this new “Duo is glitching” thing, but it keeps forcibly changing my app icon back to the new one (the one shown in the App Store right now) every time I complete a lesson. I have the streak icon unlocked and my family pays for the super plan that comes with an icon as well, and I usually use the super icon, but now the app is trying its hardest to force the new icon onto my screen. I shouldn’t have to go back and manually change it to the icon we literally pay money for every time I finish a lesson. It was understandable the first time, like I thought maybe it was just a one-off joke, but after every lesson is way too much.

Also, I’ve noticed some of the lessons got rearranged over time to have a more coherent flow, I do like that part, but I came across a pretty badly placed lesson today in the Japanese course. I’m just recently coming back to Japanese so I’m kind of near the beginning (my score is like 6 or so, super low). The unit I’m in has me learning stuff like hello, good evening, nice to meet you etc. And then out of nowhere there’s a speaking lesson with comparatively complex sentences and words that never got introduced to me (ex: “can I borrow this smartphone”). You can’t blame me for getting the questions wrong when I didn’t understand a single word of the longer phrases. If any Duolingo devs are reading this, please look into rearranging the lessons again, the order is still far from perfect.

title: Duolingo is amazing!
text:
(PLEASE READ!)

I’m not being paid for this

Ive been learning German on Duolingo for over a year now (current 489 days!) and it’s been extremely useful. I was able to figure out what a family was saying in German whilst I was on a vacation, and can easily translate basic phrases to my friends/family. Other people say the German course is wrong with the dialects and everything, but I say it’s pretty great. I totally recommend it to anyone looking to learn math, Spanish, English, and/or German. The French Course is also absolutely amazing!

 Chess, is another story. Yes ive learned a lot in the past 3 weeks but ELO barely goes up if you win against Oscar, but if “Chess Daddy” (a recent phrase theyve added) beats YOU, the ELO goes down IMMENSELY. 

If you want to learn chess, you need to have patience. 

Super Duolingo/ Duolingo Max IS EXPENSIVE; but also worth it as you can do really anything with it. 

THANK YOU! ☘️

title: They just screwed you if you're not on a subscription
text: Used to be hearts if you got something wrong. Now they're doing energy. You you burn one energy per question. title: Couldn’t cancel subscription
text: Ever since I started a new account with Duolingo after the ai revamp, my experience has been awful. I was offered the free trial for super Duolingo like everyone has but right after I started the trial, I went to manage subscription and it took me to a blank page. I thought there was a problem with the app so I reinstalled it but the same thing kept happening. Even when I logged out and back in. I decided to try from my computer thinking the app was the problem but even trying to login from the website took my to a blank page. I can’t even go the help center or submit a complaint. The only way I managed to FINALLY was from a link through ChatGPT. They asked for screenshots and gave me a reference number in case something else pops up but no answer to stopping the subscription from going through. I even pleaded for them to manually stop the subscription because I don’t $105 dollars to my name. Now I’ve had to keep my card locked for days while I keep seeing notifications on a transaction being (rightfully) declined. Once this gets resolved (hopefully) I’m NEVER using Duolingo again and I regret coming back in the first place.

title: There was a programming error
text:
In one of the iterations from several day ago there were three or four choices to choose from. I pressed the one I wanted, but nothing happened. The screen appeared to be dead. title: Terrific
text: Duolingo plus has helped my in my education by helping me fix my grammar mistakes in Spanish and giving me fun and efficient ways to learn while not getting distracted. title: Removed audio lessons
text:
They removed my favorite features. Phrases that mimic how you really hear language and conversations in real life. 
They were grouped by different events like shopping, going out to eat, traveling. You could listen to whole conversations not annoying and limited like the podcast or calls. 
You didn’t have to stay on the path they created. 
You could chose to focus on certain phrases instead of just words. 
Also now can not learn Tagalog anymore but have the option to Tagalog to learn English.

title: User for ten years, it’s.. fine
text:
I’ve been using Duolingo for more than ten years and I like a lot about it but it’s gotten worse over time. The lessons are boring if you’re good with languages. They’re repetitive and you can speed run them without much effort. The features are becoming more expensive. The app with ads is almost unusable. I hope there will be more content and more opportunities to incorporate grammar tables and references into the courses. I liked it when you could pop out the grammar or conjugation of a verb but now that feature has been monetized as well. I don’t know that I’ll keep using this app long term if it continues to monetize important learning features and spam
Users with repetitive lessons. Grammar shouldn’t be paywalled, specially when you already pay a subscription fee.

text:

Then the only choices are to buy Super, spend 600 gems, or ditch the lesson and come back tomorrow. I guess this is their newest way to harass free users into paying up for Super (on top of the annoying ads, which I know are part of the free app system).

title: Duolingo has no black male character. Why?
text:
Folks in the Duoverse would rather make friends with a giant grizzly bear than a black man?! The app’s inclusion of so many other cultures makes this exclusion of black men look purposeful. For instance, Duolingo constantly boasts that it preserves endangered languages like Hawaiian and Navajo — which is beautiful — but why can’t Duo be bothered to teach any fully African languages? Hausa is spoken by 50 million people and is “the most important indigenous lingua franca in West and Central Africa,” according to Encyclopaedia Britannica. But Hausa is not offered on Duolingo. Neither is the Yoruba language, which is spoken by 47 million people. Yes, Duolingo teaches Swahili — a language largely rooted in Arabic, which was spread through Africa by colonization and conquest by people from outside Africa — but Duolingo offers no other African language, and no fully indigenous African language. Those facts, combined with Duolingo’s lack of any black male character, makes Duolingo appear to be a tool of cultural colonialism: The app primarily teaches the languages of Western and Western-style industrial nations, and thus encourages users to communicate with those nations, but Duolingo simultaneously subtly teaches users to think that Africa and Africans are unimportant, invisible, and not worth talking with about anything. Such cultural colonialism ignores the hugely important contributions that Africans — and the African diaspora — have made to global culture. Black people built the Pyramids, were heroes and heroines of Bible stories, invented important science and art, and gave the world its modern musical soundtrack. So, Duo needs to respect blacks more, including black men. During American slavery, enslavers intentionally worked to make black men powerless and invisible, while those enslavers r-ped black women and trained them to despise their own men. Perhaps not coincidentally, Duolingo’s sole black female character, Bea, resembles the slave mammy Aunt Jemima, wearing a similar headscarf, and has no romantic interest in black men. On another note: Why can’t Duolingo users save their blocks of double XP for when folks actually have time to use it?

Update: Some glitch in Duolingo has prevented the app from starting on my phone for the past three days straight, so that I appear to have lost my 620-day streak through no fault of my own. This needs to be fixed, so that my streak can be restored.

title: Potential Revisions/Additions
text:
Vikram’s community hotline (Spanish) is a great idea, but Vikram’s voice is painful to listen to. It’s unemotional and stale. Otherwise, it’s fine.

I’d also implement an AI-powered conversation activity. Maybe have an objective you need to reach through talking to a character. Try and order coffee with an impatient barista or explain to a teacher why you don’t have your homework… there’s a few things you could do with this general idea.

I also think it’d be nice to be able to pick and choose vocabulary “sets:” words that are related in an attempt to personalize learning in both efforts and goals. You could have a sports set, a cafe set, a hotel set, you get the idea. They’re all words meant for that area of your life. This is just to prevent learning words for “snail,” “ceiling,” and “wrist” at the same time, if you know what I mean. I’d rather learn “quickly,” “late,” and other more useful words.

I understand that some of these are intensive extras, but I just think it’d help make the app more enjoyable and educational.




Edit: I’m writing this much later. I just wanted to say that with longer and longer streaks, I feel a growing sense of burnout. Going back every day even when you don’t want to can be extraordinarily tough, and it feels almost punishing rather than encouraging. Maybe offer a system where you can keep a streak by doing a lesson each week? Maybe the streak flame dies down and looks smaller and less bright but doesn’t go out, you know? There’s a reason summer break is so cherished among students, and I feel that this feature would at least allow me personally to take a mental break amidst school and sports. Just spitballing - hope you see this

title: Potential Revisions/Additions
text:
Vikram’s community hotline (Spanish) is a great idea, but Vikram’s voice is painful to listen to. It’s unemotional and stale. Otherwise, it’s fine.

I’d also implement an AI-powered conversation activity. Maybe have an objective you need to reach through talking to a character. Try and order coffee with an impatient barista or explain to a teacher why you don’t have your homework… there’s a few things you could do with this general idea.

I also think it’d be nice to be able to pick and choose vocabulary “sets:” words that are related in an attempt to personalize learning in both efforts and goals. You could have a sports set, a cafe set, a hotel set, you get the idea. They’re all words meant for that area of your life. This is just to prevent learning words for “snail,” “ceiling,” and “wrist” at the same time, if you know what I mean. I’d rather learn “quickly,” “late,” and other more useful words.

I understand that some of these are intensive extras, but I just think it’d help make the app more enjoyable and educational.




Edit: I’m writing this much later. I just wanted to say that with longer and longer streaks, I feel a growing sense of burnout. Going back every day even when you don’t want to can be extraordinarily tough, and it feels almost punishing rather than encouraging. Maybe offer a system where you can keep a streak by doing a lesson each week? Maybe the streak flame dies down and looks smaller and less bright but doesn’t go out, you know? There’s a reason summer break is so cherished among students, and I feel that this feature would at least allow me personally to take a mental break amidst school and sports. Just spitballing - hope you see this

title: I love it, but they didn’t remind me to cancel free trial when they said they will.
text:
I love Duolingo, I’m learning Japanese, but when I got the free trial for Super Duolingo, they said that they will remind me 2 days before the trial ends, but they didn’t remind me. They didn’t even send a notification or email. When I looked in my email I saw that they charged me ~$100 for the yearly subscription plan as a receipt from Apple without reminding me that the trial was almost over. I requested a refund ASAP from Apple, and it’s pending as of now and will see if it’ll be successful soon. To be honest, it’s my fault that I forgot to cancel, but they didn’t remind me to since I was expecting a reminder from them when they said they will.

Other than that I love the app. It’s fun and engaging, which is what I love about it. It also has a streak system, which keeps me committed to learning languages. I would recommend getting it (without subscription ofc, unless you want to pay for it).

I won’t be getting the paid subscription though. 

*(P.S., Be careful and remember to cancel the free trial
If you don’t want to pay for the subscription)*

title: Coding would help - Request to add a learning Course.
text: Hi there, I’ve used Duolingo for a little while and have obtained a 79 day streak. It’s fun, interactive and actually helps learn. I’ve learned French, a little bit of Japanese, Music, and Chess. I truly feel if somehow, one day Duolingo can add a Coding course, it would not only make the app so much better, but also more approachable to a larger group. I know Duolingo is meant to be a primarily language teaching app, but if you’re adding Math and Chess. Why not consider Coding? It can expand the app and be more fulfilling to others who want to learn. Thank you for taking your time to read this, and if any developers do see this message, please consider. Thank you and goodbye for now.

title: Hearts felt like a game; Energy feels like it's no longer a free app
text:
Loved this app when I started using it years ago, but the updates don't feel like improvements so much as "shrinkflation". 

I already disliked how much they're pushing AI and paid upgrades, but the switch from "Hearts" to "Energy" made it way worse. Hearts felt like a fun part of the game, and encouraged me to avoid careless mistakes. Energy just drains away regardless and feels like old "shareware" programs. 

I guess you could say it's rather "disheartening" :P

title: I love it, but they didn’t remind me to cancel free trial when they said they will.
text:
I love Duolingo, I’m learning Japanese, but when I got the free trial for Super Duolingo, they said that they will remind me 2 days before the trial ends, but they didn’t remind me. They didn’t even send a notification or email. When I looked in my email I saw that they charged me ~$100 for the yearly subscription plan as a receipt from Apple without reminding me that the trial was almost over. I requested a refund ASAP from Apple, and it’s pending as of now and will see if it’ll be successful soon. To be honest, it’s my fault that I forgot to cancel, but they didn’t remind me to since I was expecting a reminder from them when they said they will.

Other than that I love the app. It’s fun and engaging, which is what I love about it. It also has a streak system, which keeps me committed to learning languages. I would recommend getting it (without subscription ofc, unless you want to pay for it).

I won’t be getting the paid subscription though. 

*(P.S., Be careful and remember to cancel the free trial
If you don’t want to pay for the subscription)*

Was dedicated to spanish but now I’m questioning learning a language in the first place.
